text: The left lobe of the liver is visibly obscuring the surgical view during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon attempts to position a subxiphoid instrument beneath the left lobe of the liver to retract it, facilitating dissection. The instrument is maneuvered to release the duodenal attachments.

The surgeon begins dissection at the line of Riviera sulcus at the bottom of the gallbladder, opening the peritoneum near its junction with the liver bed, and proceeds to dissect towards the patient's right or lateral side of the gallbladder.
The surgeon observes the gallbladder, which appears edematous due to previous pressure buildup from cholangitis.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the cystic duct, which is becoming visible, aiming to expose the gallbladder wall and the junction of the cystic duct in preparation for a cholangiogram.

The surgeon uses a retic catheter with a balloon tip to perform a cholangiogram. An otomy is made on the gallbladder wall, and a fourteen gauge angiocath is placed in the right upper quadrant below the costal margin. The balloon catheter is inserted into the cystic duct, inflated, and the cholangiogram is conducted.

The cholangiogram confirms the proper identification of the cystic duct, showing no filling defects in the common bile duct. The imaging reveals good flow into the duodenum and up the right and left common hepatic ducts.
The surgeon clips the cystic duct following confirmation of its identification and proceeds to divide it, marking a key step in the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon notes that the cystic artery has not been clearly visualized yet due to limited dissection performed earlier to facilitate the cholangiogram.

The surgeon dissects the cystic artery, which is now clearly visible, noting its bifurcation. The anterior limb of the artery is being isolated, while the posterior medial branch is observed during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully removes the gallbladder from the liver bed, staying within the adventitial plane. Despite the presence of edema, which aids in identifying the dissection plane, the procedure proceeds smoothly without causing a hole in the gallbladder, avoiding the spillage of small stones.
The surgeon moves the camera to a right upper quadrant trocar and introduces a retrieval bag through the umbilical trocar, which is used to extract the gallbladder.
